query_normalized: llmxive follow-up: extending "guava: an effective and universal harness for embodied manipulation" computer science
rationale: This paper satisfies criteria (a) and (b) as it investigates the same core mechanism (bridging the "seeing-to-doing" or perception-action gap in embodied manipulation) and operates on the same domain (general robotic manipulation using VLMs), providing essential prior work on universal harnesses and generalization strategies that the user's follow-up research would need to cite.
